topic: A historical event and its later cultural memory
reader: general reader seeking a concise but careful explanation of what happened and why it matters
use_context: A historical-cultural explainer that distinguishes event chronology from later interpretation and memory.
known_failure_modes: blending modern interpretation into the historical record
omitting why later memory changed the event's meaning
required_claims: how later culture reframed the event
prohibited_overclaims: does not claim perfect consensus where there is none
does not project modern values onto all participants

source_packets.title: Primary source excerpt
source_packets.summary: Provides direct evidence about the event's immediate context and the language used at the time.

findings.statement: The immediate record is limited to a narrow local context rather than a universal one.
findings.decision_note: Use this to keep chronology grounded in the actual source limits.
failure_modes: reading a local record as if it described the entire movement or era
misunderstandings: assuming one source captures the whole event

findings.statement: The wording in the source leaves room for more than one interpretation.
findings.decision_note: Call out ambiguity explicitly instead of smoothing it over.
failure_modes: presenting ambiguous phrasing as settled fact
misunderstandings: assuming the primary source is self-explanatory

source_packets.title: Cultural history essay
source_packets.summary: Explains how the event was later remembered, reinterpreted, and used in public culture.

findings.statement: Later cultural memory emphasized symbolism more than the original local circumstances.
findings.decision_note: This supports the distinction between what happened and what people later made of it.
misunderstandings: assuming later symbolic meaning was present from the start

findings.statement: Scholars disagree on which later reinterpretation became dominant first.
findings.decision_note: Mention the disagreement rather than pretending there is one clean line of influence.
misunderstandings: assuming the literature converges on one timeline
